sections.claim: The FA4 B200 white-box predictor correctly identifies compute-bound regimes but over-predicts memory/TMA dominance for medium-size problems.
evidence: 12 saturation configs were profiled with NCU SpeedOfLight; 12 returned valid compute/memory counters.
Coarse accuracy (compute vs memory) was 66.7% overall: 100% for mma, 100% for mufu, 66.7% for hbm, and 0% for tma.

The three TMA-targeted configs (batch=16/16/2048-128, batch=8/32/4096-128, batch=4/64/2048-64) were predicted as memory/tma but measured as compute/mma by NCU, with compute_pct 53-72% and memory_pct 12-26%.
One HBM-targeted config (batch=32/16/512-64) was predicted as memory/hbm but measured as compute/mma (compute_pct 32.6%, memory_pct 15.7%).
implications: The predictor's TMA bandwidth model (tma_bytes_per_ns=240, factor=0.65) appears too low relative to effective compute overlap, making TMA time dominate when hardware is still compute-limited.
The coarse compute/memory split is the most trustworthy comparison; fine labels for memory stages are derived from the model itself and should be treated as diagnostic, not ground truth.
L2 and SMEM labels cannot be independently validated because the current workload model never makes them the dominant memory stage.
limitations: NCU SpeedOfLight only resolves compute vs memory, not individual memory hierarchies.
Single-GPU profiling ignores batching, multi-GPU, and concurrency effects.
Only bf16 precision was tested; fp8 and fp16 may shift bottlenecks.
sections.next_action: Investigate whether the TMA time should be overlapped with MMA rather than added to a max() roofline, or whether the TMA bandwidth constant needs recalibration.

summary: Analysis of saturation experiment: model is reliable for compute-bound regimes but needs TMA/compute overlap revision for memory-bound regimes.
title: Analysis Finding: Bottleneck-Saturation Diagnostic for the FA4 B200 Predictor
